sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A communications lab randomized 240 adults to one of four text reminders about influenza vaccination: a gain-framed message emphasizing benefits, a loss-framed message emphasizing risks of not vaccinating, a peer-norm message stating that most people in the participant's age group had already scheduled the shot, or an informational control message. Participants rated intention to schedule vaccination on a 1–10 scale immediately afterward. Two weeks later, researchers checked whether an appointment had been scheduled. Mean intention scores were 6.2 for gain framing, 7.1 for loss framing, 8.0 for the peer-norm message, and 5.4 for the control. Scheduling rates were 28%, 34%, 46%, and 22%, respectively. The peer-norm effect was strongest among participants who reported checking social media at least once per hour.
questions.stem: According to Figure 1, which condition produced the highest scheduling rate?

explanations.A: Correct. The peer-norm condition shows the tallest scheduling bar at 46%, exceeding the loss-framed condition at 34%, the gain-framed condition at 28%, and the control at 22%. The figure therefore indicates that emphasizing what peers are doing produced the largest behavioral response.
explanations.B: The gain-framed message improved intention relative to control, but its scheduling rate is only 28%, not the highest value in the figure.
explanations.C: Loss framing outperformed gain framing, yet its 34% scheduling rate still falls below the peer-norm condition.
explanations.D: The control group had the lowest behavioral response at 22%, so it cannot be the condition with the highest scheduling rate.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean intention ratings and two-week scheduling rates after exposure to four vaccine reminder messages.

questions.stem: The peer-norm message most directly relies on which social-psychological principle?

explanations.B: Correct. Telling participants that most peers have already scheduled vaccination uses perceived group behavior as a cue. That is normative social influence: people shift attitudes or behavior to align with what they believe others like them are doing.
explanations.A: Cognitive dissonance involves tension between beliefs and actions, but the passage manipulates perceived norms rather than inconsistency.
explanations.C: Classical conditioning pairs a neutral stimulus with an unconditioned stimulus. The text reminder does not describe repeated stimulus pairings.
explanations.D: The mere-exposure effect refers to greater liking after repeated exposure. The passage concerns social norms, not familiarity.

questions.stem: Because the reminders were randomly assigned, the study best supports which conclusion?

choices.C: The reminder condition likely contributed to differences in scheduling behavior
choices.A: Hourly social-media use was caused by the reminder condition
choices.B: Participants with stronger health habits were evenly distributed because researchers measured them directly
choices.D: The peer-norm effect proves long-term vaccine adherence will improve

explanations.C: Correct. Random assignment makes the reminder condition the main systematic difference between groups, so group differences in immediate intention and short-term scheduling can be interpreted as effects of the messaging condition. It does not eliminate every unknown factor forever, but it strengthens causal inference for the measured outcomes.
explanations.A: Social-media habits were a preexisting participant characteristic, not something created by the randomization.
explanations.B: Randomization can balance measured and unmeasured factors on average, but the passage never says health habits were directly measured and confirmed equal.
explanations.D: The study only tracked whether an appointment was scheduled two weeks later, not long-term adherence across seasons.

questions.stem: If a clinician wanted to use the same mechanism in another context, which strategy would be most analogous?

choices.D: Telling patients that most others their age complete colorectal cancer screening on time
choices.A: Explaining the chemistry of vaccine preservatives in more detail
choices.B: Playing a chime whenever a patient enters the clinic
choices.C: Giving every patient the identical neutral reminder sheet

explanations.D: Correct. This strategy applies the same norm-based mechanism by highlighting the behavior of similar others. It attempts to motivate action by making the desired behavior appear common and expected within a relevant peer group.
explanations.A: More factual detail may educate patients, but it does not primarily use social norms.
explanations.B: A chime is a sensory cue and does not communicate what peers are doing.
explanations.C: A neutral reminder does not leverage the persuasive advantage seen in the peer-norm condition.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the interpretation that social-media habits amplified the peer-norm effect?

choices.A: Heavy social-media users in the control condition did not show unusually high scheduling, but heavy users in the peer-norm condition did
choices.B: Heavy social-media users preferred the gain-framed message to the loss-framed message by a small margin
choices.C: Light social-media users reported more hours of sleep than heavy social-media users
choices.D: Participants with hourly social-media use had lower baseline knowledge about influenza

explanations.A: Correct. If heavy users only show especially high scheduling when the message contains a peer norm, that pattern supports moderation by social-media habits rather than a general tendency for heavy users to schedule vaccines regardless of condition.
explanations.B: A small preference between two persuasive conditions does not show that social-media use specifically amplified the peer-norm pathway.
explanations.C: Sleep differences do not address whether social-media habits changed responsiveness to the norm message.
explanations.D: Lower baseline knowledge could matter, but by itself it does not show that the norm message worked especially well among heavy users.

questions.passage: A campus health center tested anti-vaping posters among 180 college students. One poster used fear appeals by highlighting severe lung injury and addiction. A second paired the same risk information with a self-efficacy message explaining three concrete steps for quitting or refusing vaping. A third poster provided neutral facts about product ingredients. After viewing the posters, students rated perceived risk and confidence in their ability to avoid vaping on 1–10 scales. One-month follow-up surveys recorded self-reported vaping. Mean confidence scores were 4.1 for the fear-only group, 7.3 for the fear-plus-self-efficacy group, and 5.0 for the facts-only group. Vaping rates at follow-up were 24%, 14%, and 21%, respectively.
questions.stem: Which condition in Figure 2 combined the highest confidence with the lowest follow-up vaping?

explanations.B: Correct. The fear-plus-self-efficacy condition shows the highest confidence score, 7.3, and the lowest reported vaping rate, 14%. That pairing stands out relative to the other two conditions, which have lower confidence and higher vaping at follow-up.
explanations.A: Fear-only messages elevated risk salience, but this group had lower confidence and a higher vaping rate than the combined condition.
explanations.C: The facts-only poster produced middling confidence and a higher vaping rate than the combined condition.
explanations.D: One condition clearly fits both criteria, so this response is contradicted by the figure.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Self-efficacy ratings and one-month vaping rates after exposure to three anti-vaping posters.

questions.stem: Why would adding a self-efficacy component be expected to improve behavior change?

choices.C: People are more likely to act on a warning when they believe they can successfully perform the recommended behavior
choices.B: Self-efficacy reduces the need for any social support
choices.D: Confidence messages work only because they distract from health risks

explanations.C: Correct. Self-efficacy theory predicts that warnings work better when people also believe they can carry out the recommended response. The combined poster did not just increase perceived risk; it also provided actionable steps, which likely made avoidance or quitting seem achievable.
explanations.A: Threat information can increase concern, but it does not automatically remove dependence or craving.
explanations.B: People can still benefit from support even when they feel capable; self-efficacy does not make support unnecessary.
explanations.D: The value of the message is not distraction. The poster appears more effective because it pairs risk with a feasible response.

questions.stem: Which variable is the dependent variable in the one-month follow-up analysis?

choices.D: Whether students reported vaping at one month
choices.B: Whether the student was in college
choices.C: The number of steps listed on the poster

explanations.D: Correct. At follow-up, the outcome being measured is whether students reported vaping. That behavior is the dependent variable because it is the response assessed after the experimental manipulation of poster type.
explanations.A: Poster type is the independent variable manipulated by the researchers.
explanations.B: All participants were college students, so that characteristic was not the measured outcome in the follow-up analysis.
explanations.C: The steps listed on the poster are part of the intervention content rather than the behavioral outcome.

questions.stem: A public-health campaign based on these findings should most likely emphasize which approach?

choices.A: Pair warnings about harm with clear, doable actions people can take immediately
choices.B: Use only the most frightening images possible
choices.C: Avoid discussing risk and focus on ingredients alone
choices.D: Replace posters with messages that simply say vaping is common

explanations.A: Correct. The combined condition performed best because it linked risk information to concrete, manageable steps. That suggests the most effective campaign would preserve concern about harm while also showing people what to do next.
explanations.B: Fear alone produced the lowest confidence and did not reduce vaping as much as the combined approach.
explanations.C: Facts alone were less effective than the condition that included risk plus self-efficacy support.
explanations.D: Telling students a behavior is common could normalize vaping rather than discourage it.

questions.stem: Which additional finding would most weaken a purely causal interpretation of the poster effect on vaping?

choices.B: Students in the fear-plus-self-efficacy condition already had the lowest baseline vaping before viewing the posters
choices.A: Students in all three groups spent similar time looking at the posters
choices.C: Perceived risk increased in both fear-based conditions
choices.D: The sample included both daily and occasional vapers

explanations.B: Correct. If the most successful group already had the lowest vaping rate before the intervention, then preexisting group differences could partly account for the follow-up pattern. That would weaken the claim that the poster itself produced the entire effect.
explanations.A: Equal viewing time removes one possible confound rather than weakening the interpretation.
explanations.C: A rise in perceived risk is compatible with the poster mechanism and does not undermine causal inference.
explanations.D: Including different types of vapers adds heterogeneity, but it does not by itself show that group differences predated the manipulation.
